comment_text.ud4.8:2.2: Ānandajoti has “venture”, Ṭhānissaro has “dare”, but a similar phrase at Kd 8:12.1.5 clearly has the weaker sense of “able”, per Ireland and commentary (ussahasīti sakkosi).
comment_text.ud4.8:2.4: Accepting the variant kiṁ mayā sakkā, which is found in all similar passages. Reading, as Ṭhānissaro and Ānandajoti do, “what can I not do” is pleasingly dramatic, but not very idiomatic for Pali. Far more likely a simple reinforcement is meant. Commentary ignores this phrase, implying it thought there was nothing unusual.
comment_text.ud4.8:6.3: The idiom ime hi nāma appears mildly emphatic in a disparaging way. Compare kathaṁ hi nāma just below.

root_text.ud4.8:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.ud4.8:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena bhagavā sakkato hoti garukato mānito pūjito apacito lābhī cīvarapiṇḍapātasenāsanagilānapaccayabhesajjaparikkhārānaṁ.
root_text.ud4.8:1.4: Bhikkhusaṅghopi sakkato hoti garukato mānito pūjito apacito lābhī cīvarapiṇḍapātasenāsanagilānapaccayabhesajjaparikkhārānaṁ.
root_text.ud4.8:1.5: Aññatitthiyā pana paribbājakā asakkatā honti agarukatā amānitā apūjitā anapacitā na lābhino cīvarapiṇḍapātasenāsanagilānapaccayabhesajjaparikkhārānaṁ.
root_text.ud4.8:2.1: Atha kho te aññatitthiyā paribbājakā bhagavato sakkāraṁ asahamānā bhikkhusaṅghassa ca yena sundarī paribbājikā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā sundariṁ paribbājikaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.ud4.8:3.2: “Evaṁ, ayyā”ti kho sundarī paribbājikā tesaṁ aññatitthiyānaṁ paribbājakānaṁ paṭissutvā abhikkhaṇaṁ jetavanaṁ agamāsi.
root_text.ud4.8:4.2: “vodiṭṭhā kho sundarī paribbājikā bahujanena abhikkhaṇaṁ jetavanaṁ gacchantī”ti.
root_text.ud4.8:4.3: Atha naṁ jīvitā voropetvā tattheva jetavanassa parikhākūpe nikkhipitvā yena rājā pasenadi kosalo tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā rājānaṁ pasenadiṁ kosalaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.ud4.8:4.4: “yā sā, mahārāja, sundarī paribbājikā; sā no na dissatī”ti.
root_text.ud4.8:5.1: Atha kho te aññatitthiyā paribbājakā jetavanaṁ vicinitvā yathānikkhittaṁ parikhākūpā uddharitvā mañcakaṁ āropetvā sāvatthiṁ pavesetvā rathiyāya rathiyaṁ siṅghāṭakena siṅghāṭakaṁ upasaṅkamitvā manusse ujjhāpesuṁ:
root_text.ud4.8:6.2: Alajjino ime samaṇā sakyaputtiyā dussīlā pāpadhammā musāvādino abrahmacārino.
root_text.ud4.8:6.3: Ime hi nāma dhammacārino samacārino brahmacārino saccavādino sīlavanto kalyāṇadhammā paṭijānissanti.
root_text.ud4.8:6.4: Natthi imesaṁ sāmaññaṁ, natthi imesaṁ brahmaññaṁ.
root_text.ud4.8:6.5: Naṭṭhaṁ imesaṁ sāmaññaṁ, naṭṭhaṁ imesaṁ brahmaññaṁ.
root_text.ud4.8:6.6: Kuto imesaṁ sāmaññaṁ, kuto imesaṁ brahmaññaṁ?
root_text.ud4.8:6.7: Apagatā ime sāmaññā, apagatā ime brahmaññā.
root_text.ud4.8:6.8: Kathañhi nāma puriso purisakiccaṁ karitvā itthiṁ jīvitā voropessatī”ti.
root_text.ud4.8:7.1: Tena kho pana samayena sāvatthiyaṁ manussā bhikkhū disvā asabbhāhi pharusāhi vācāhi akkosanti paribhāsanti rosanti vihesanti:
root_text.ud4.8:8.1: “Alajjino ime samaṇā sakyaputtiyā dussīlā pāpadhammā musāvādino abrahmacārino.
root_text.ud4.8:8.2: Ime hi nāma dhammacārino samacārino brahmacārino saccavādino sīlavanto kalyāṇadhammā paṭijānissanti.
root_text.ud4.8:8.3: Natthi imesaṁ sāmaññaṁ, natthi imesaṁ brahmaññaṁ.
root_text.ud4.8:8.4: Naṭṭhaṁ imesaṁ sāmaññaṁ, naṭṭhaṁ imesaṁ brahmaññaṁ.
root_text.ud4.8:8.5: Kuto imesaṁ sāmaññaṁ, kuto imesaṁ brahmaññaṁ?
root_text.ud4.8:8.6: Apagatā ime sāmaññā, apagatā ime brahmaññā.
root_text.ud4.8:8.7: Kathañhi nāma puriso purisakiccaṁ karitvā itthiṁ jīvitā voropessatī”ti.
root_text.ud4.8:9.1: Atha kho sambahulā bhikkhū pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya sāvatthiṁ piṇḍāya pāvisiṁsu.
root_text.ud4.8:9.2: Sāvatthiyaṁ piṇḍāya caritvā pacchābhattaṁ piṇḍapātapaṭikkantā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu. Ekamantaṁ nisinnā kho te bhikkhū bhagavantaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.ud4.8:10.1: “Etarahi, bhante, sāvatthiyaṁ manussā bhikkhū disvā asabbhāhi pharusāhi vācāhi akkosanti paribhāsanti rosanti vihesanti:
root_text.ud4.8:10.2: ‘alajjino ime samaṇā sakyaputtiyā dussīlā pāpadhammā musāvādino abrahmacārino.
root_text.ud4.8:10.3: Ime hi nāma dhammacārino samacārino brahmacārino saccavādino sīlavanto kalyāṇadhammā paṭijānissanti.
root_text.ud4.8:10.4: Natthi imesaṁ sāmaññaṁ, natthi imesaṁ brahmaññaṁ.
root_text.ud4.8:10.5: Naṭṭhaṁ imesaṁ sāmaññaṁ, naṭṭhaṁ imesaṁ brahmaññaṁ.
root_text.ud4.8:10.6: Kuto imesaṁ sāmaññaṁ, kuto imesaṁ brahmaññaṁ?
root_text.ud4.8:10.7: Apagatā ime sāmaññā, apagatā ime brahmaññā.
root_text.ud4.8:10.8: Kathañhi nāma puriso purisakiccaṁ karitvā itthiṁ jīvitā voropessatī’”ti.
root_text.ud4.8:11.1: “Neso, bhikkhave, saddo ciraṁ bhavissati sattāhameva bhavissati. Sattāhassa accayena antaradhāyissati.
root_text.ud4.8:11.2: Tena hi, bhikkhave, ye manussā bhikkhū disvā asabbhāhi pharusāhi vācāhi akkosanti paribhāsanti rosanti vihesanti, te tumhe imāya gāthāya paṭicodetha:
root_text.ud4.8:12.2: Yo vāpi katvā na karomi cāha;
root_text.ud4.8:13.1: Atha kho te bhikkhū bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ pariyāpuṇitvā ye manussā bhikkhū disvā asabbhāhi pharusāhi vācāhi akkosanti paribhāsanti rosanti vihesanti te imāya gāthāya paṭicodenti:
root_text.ud4.8:14.2: Yo vāpi katvā na karomi cāha;
root_text.ud4.8:15.2: “akārakā ime samaṇā sakyaputtiyā, nayimehi kataṁ, sapantime samaṇā sakyaputtiyā”ti.
root_text.ud4.8:15.3: Neva so saddo ciraṁ ahosi. Sattāhameva ahosi. Sattāhassa accayena antaradhāyi.
root_text.ud4.8:16.1: Atha kho sambahulā bhikkhū yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁsu. Ekamantaṁ nisinnā kho te bhikkhū bhagavato etadavocuṁ:
root_text.ud4.8:17.3: ‘neso, bhikkhave, saddo ciraṁ bhavissati. Sattāhameva bhavissati. Sattāhassa accayena antaradhāyissatī’ti.
root_text.ud4.8:18.1: Atha kho bhagavā etamatthaṁ viditvā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ udānaṁ udānesi:

translation_text.ud4.8:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.ud4.8:1.3: Now at that time the Buddha was honored, respected, revered, venerated, and esteemed. And he received robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.
translation_text.ud4.8:1.4: And the mendicant Saṅgha was also honored, respected, revered, venerated, and esteemed. And they received robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.
translation_text.ud4.8:1.5: But the wanderers who followed other religions were not honored, respected, revered, venerated, and esteemed. And they didn’t receive robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.
translation_text.ud4.8:2.1: Then those wanderers who followed other religions, unable to bear the esteem of the mendicant Sangha, approached the female wanderer Sundarī and said,
translation_text.ud4.8:2.2: “Sister, are you able to do something for the welfare of your kin?”
translation_text.ud4.8:2.5: I’d even give my life for the welfare of my kin.”
translation_text.ud4.8:3.1: “Well then, sister, frequently visit Jeta’s Grove.”
translation_text.ud4.8:3.2: “Yes, masters,” she replied, and did as they asked.
translation_text.ud4.8:4.2: that Sundarī had been clearly seen by many people frequently visiting Jeta’s Grove,
translation_text.ud4.8:4.3: they killed her and dumped her in the ditch around Jeta’s Grove. Then they went to see King Pasenadi of Kosala and said to him,
translation_text.ud4.8:4.4: “Great king, we cannot find the female wanderer Sundarī.”
translation_text.ud4.8:4.5: “But where do you suspect she is?”
translation_text.ud4.8:5.1: So the wanderers searched Jeta’s Grove. They pulled her body up from the ditch where they had dumped it, and lifted it on a bier. Having entered Sāvatthī, they went from street to street and from square to square, complaining to people:
translation_text.ud4.8:6.1: “See the deed of the ascetics who follow the Sakyan!
translation_text.ud4.8:6.2: Shameless are these ascetics who follow the Sakyan, immoral and wicked. They are liars and unchaste.
translation_text.ud4.8:6.3: Sure, they claim to be of principled and moral conduct, to be chaste, truthful, ethical, and of good character.
translation_text.ud4.8:6.4: But they have no asceticism, no spirituality.
translation_text.ud4.8:6.5: Asceticism and spirituality are lost to them!
translation_text.ud4.8:6.6: Where is their asceticism, where their spirituality?
translation_text.ud4.8:6.7: They have abandoned asceticism and spirituality!
translation_text.ud4.8:6.8: How on earth can a man, having done a man’s business, kill a woman!”
translation_text.ud4.8:7.1: Then at that time when the people of Sāvatthī saw the mendicants they abused and insulted them with rude, harsh words:
translation_text.ud4.8:8.1: “Shameless are these ascetics who follow the Sakyan, immoral, wicked, liars, and fake chastes.
translation_text.ud4.8:8.2: Sure, they claim to be of principled and moral conduct, to be chaste, truthful, ethical, and of good character.
translation_text.ud4.8:8.3: But they have no asceticism, no spirituality.
translation_text.ud4.8:8.4: Asceticism and spirituality are lost to them!
translation_text.ud4.8:8.5: Where is their asceticism, where their spirituality?
translation_text.ud4.8:8.6: They have abandoned asceticism and spirituality!
translation_text.ud4.8:8.7: How on earth can a man, having done a man’s business, kill a woman!”
translation_text.ud4.8:9.1: Then several mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, entered Sāvatthī for alms.
translation_text.ud4.8:9.2: Then, after the meal, when they returned from almsround, they went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and told him what was happening.
translation_text.ud4.8:11.1: “That rumor, mendicants, won’t last long. It will only be seven days, then it will vanish.
translation_text.ud4.8:11.2: So you may respond to those critics with this verse:
translation_text.ud4.8:12.2: as does one who denies what they did.
translation_text.ud4.8:12.3: Both are equal in the hereafter,
translation_text.ud4.8:13.1: The mendicants memorized that verse in the Buddha’s presence, then used it to respond to those critics.
translation_text.ud4.8:15.2: “These ascetics who follow the Sakyan didn’t do it, it was not done by them, they swear it.”
translation_text.ud4.8:15.3: That rumor didn’t last long. It was seven days, then it vanished.
translation_text.ud4.8:16.1: Then several mendicants went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him,
translation_text.ud4.8:17.2: How well said this was by the Buddha:
translation_text.ud4.8:17.3: ‘That rumor, mendicants, won’t last long. It will only be seven days, then it will vanish.’
translation_text.ud4.8:18.1: Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha expressed this heartfelt sentiment:
translation_text.ud4.8:19.1: “People out of control stab with words,
translation_text.ud4.8:19.2: like they stab a tusker in battle with darts.
translation_text.ud4.8:19.3: When they hear a harsh word spoken,
translation_text.ud4.8:19.4: a mendicant should endure with no anger in heart.”

variant_text.ud4.8:2.4: mayā na sakkā → kiṁ mayā sakkā (sya-all, pts-vp-pli1)
